data.title: Terminal AI Agents with OpenClaw & OpenCode
data.summary: Build, configure, and orchestrate terminal-based AI agents using OpenClaw for personal assistance and OpenCode for automated coding, covering installation, memory, skills, multi-agent pipelines, and production deployment.
data.description: Build, configure, and orchestrate terminal-based AI agents using OpenClaw for personal assistance and OpenCode for automated coding, covering installation, memory, skills, multi-agent pipelines, and production deployment.

nodes.title: Chapter 1: Foundations and Environment Setup
nodes.description: Introduce the architecture of OpenClaw and OpenCode, then set up a complete local and cloud development environment.
learning_points: Explain the core concepts and differences between OpenClaw and OpenCode
Install Node.js 22, npm, and required CLI tools on macOS/Linux
Deploy OpenClaw locally and on a cloud instance
Run a minimal agent to verify the setup

sub_nodes.title: 1.1 Understanding Terminal AI Agent Architecture
sub_nodes.description: Explore the conceptual model of a terminal AI agent: context assembly, model invocation, tool execution, and state persistence. Compare OpenClaw’s personal assistant focus with OpenCode’s coding specialization.
learning_points: Define agent components: planner, executor, memory, tools
Identify use‑case alignment for OpenClaw vs OpenCode
Review high‑level data flow diagrams from official docs

sub_nodes.title: 1.2 Installing Prerequisites and Dependencies
sub_nodes.description: Step‑by‑step installation of Node.js 22 via Homebrew, npm package manager, and optional Docker for containerized agents. Includes verification commands.
learning_points: Use Homebrew to install Node.js 22 and link the binary
Verify version with node -v and npm -v
Install Docker if you plan to run agents in containers

sub_nodes.title: 1.3 Deploying OpenClaw on Local and Cloud Platforms
sub_nodes.description: Hands‑on lab to clone the OpenClaw repo, configure environment variables, and start the agent locally and on Alibaba Cloud. Includes systemd service creation.
learning_points: Clone OpenClaw repository and run npm install
Configure API keys and model endpoints in .env
Launch agent with PM2 or systemd for background operation

sub_nodes.title: 1.4 Quick‑Start Demo: Your First Terminal Agent
sub_nodes.description: Create a simple skill that echoes user input, deploy it, and interact via terminal. Emphasizes theory‑practice loop: plan → code → test → iterate.
learning_points: Define a basic skill manifest (name, trigger, actions)
Write the skill logic in JavaScript/TypeScript
Test with curl or telnet to the local agent port

nodes.title: Chapter 2: Configuration, Memory, and Third‑Party Integration
nodes.description: Master OpenClaw configuration files to shape agent personality, set up long‑term memory, and securely connect external services like Telegram and email.
learning_points: Customize agent personality via YAML/JSON configuration
Implement persistent memory using file‑based storage and vector DB
Integrate at least one third‑party messaging platform
Apply security best practices for API key management

sub_nodes.title: 2.1 Shaping Agent Personality with Config Files
sub_nodes.description: Theory: explore the schema of OpenClaw’s config (personality traits, response style). Practice: edit config.yaml to adjust tone, verbosity, and skill activation rules.
learning_points: Understand the structure of personality, toolset, and memory sections
Use JSON schema validation to catch configuration errors
Test personality changes live with a chat loop

sub_nodes.title: 2.2 Long‑Term Memory with File Store and Vector DB
sub_nodes.description: Theory: discuss memory architectures (key‑value, embeddings). Practice: enable the built‑in file store and optionally add a Pinecone or FAISS vector store for semantic recall.
learning_points: Configure memory provider in config.yaml
Generate embeddings for user‑provided documents
Query memory from a skill to retrieve past context

sub_nodes.title: 2.3 Connecting Telegram and Email as Action Tools
sub_nodes.description: Theory: examine OpenClaw’s tool interface for external APIs. Practice: register Telegram bot, set webhook, and write a skill that reads inbox and sends alerts.
learning_points: Create a Telegram bot via BotFather and capture the token
Implement OAuth2 for Gmail API access
Write a skill that posts a daily summary to a chat room

sub_nodes.title: 2.4 Security: Secrets Management and Encryption
sub_nodes.description: Theory: overview of secret storage (environment variables, HashiCorp Vault). Practice: migrate API keys to a .secrets file encrypted with GPG, and enable TLS for local endpoints.
learning_points: Move sensitive values to a separate .secrets file
Encrypt the file with GPG and load in runtime
Enforce TLS for all HTTP endpoints using self‑signed or Let’s Encrypt certificates

nodes.title: Chapter 3: Skill Development and Multi‑Agent Orchestration
nodes.description: Learn to author custom skills, reuse community modules, and orchestrate multiple agents (including Hermes and OpenCode) to build complex workflows.
learning_points: Write a custom skill from scratch with manifest, actions, and tests
Publish and consume community skills from the OpenClaw marketplace
Design a multi‑agent pipeline that delegates sub‑tasks to specialized agents
Integrate OpenCode’s coding capabilities into OpenClaw for AI‑assisted programming

sub_nodes.title: 3.1 Authoring a Custom Skill
sub_nodes.description: Theory: skill lifecycle (trigger → parse → action → response). Practice: scaffold a new skill using the CLI generator, implement a weather lookup using OpenWeatherMap, and write unit tests.
learning_points: Define skill manifest (name, triggers, parameters)
Implement action handlers with async/await
Add Jest/Playwright tests to verify behavior

sub_nodes.title: 3.2 Leveraging Community Skills and Reusable Modules
sub_nodes.description: Theory: benefits of modular, community‑driven skill libraries. Practice: install a community skill (e.g., “todo‑manager”), configure its settings, and extend it with a custom decorator.
learning_points: Search and install skills via openclaw skill install
Override default configuration parameters
Compose a skill with another skill using event‑driven hooks

sub_nodes.title: 3.3 Multi‑Agent Orchestration with OpenClaw and Hermes
sub_nodes.description: Theory: coordination patterns (hub‑and‑spoke, pipeline, fan‑out). Practice: deploy a Hermes Agent for memory‑intensive tasks, have OpenClaw delegate queries, and merge results into a unified response.
learning_points: Set up Hermes as a sidecar agent for long‑term memory
Define delegation protocols and result aggregation
Test a complex query that first asks OpenClaw, then fetches context from Hermes

sub_nodes.title: 3.4 Integrating OpenCode for AI‑Assisted Programming
sub_nodes.description: Theory: how OpenCode operates as a coding agent (plan → edit → verify). Practice: expose OpenCode as a skill within OpenClaw, run a code‑review workflow that suggests improvements, and capture feedback.
learning_points: Launch OpenCode as a subprocess and expose its CLI via skill
Create a skill that accepts a code snippet and returns analysis
Collect metrics (execution time, suggestion acceptance rate) for feedback loops

nodes.title: Chapter 4: Production Deployment, Monitoring, and Optimization
nodes.description: Take the agent from prototype to production: CI/CD pipelines, performance tuning, logging, scaling, and high‑availability strategies.
learning_points: Build automated CI/CD pipelines for skill and agent updates
Optimize latency and throughput by tuning model selection and caching
Implement centralized logging and real‑time alerting
Scale the agent using Docker Swarm or Kubernetes for high availability

sub_nodes.title: 4.1 CI/CD for AI Agents with GitHub Actions
sub_nodes.description: Theory: benefits of immutable deployments. Practice: write a GitHub Actions workflow that runs linting, unit tests, builds a Docker image, and deploys to a staging cluster on merge.
learning_points: Create a Dockerfile that bundles the agent and its skills
Add a workflow with stages: test, build, push, deploy
Use Docker layer caching to speed up builds

sub_nodes.title: 4.2 Performance Tuning: Model Selection and Caching
sub_nodes.description: Theory: trade‑offs between model size, cost, and latency. Practice: configure multi‑model fallback, implement Redis caching for frequent queries, and benchmark response times.
learning_points: Switch between lightweight and heavyweight models based on task complexity
Cache embeddings with Redis to reduce repeated computation
Collect metrics with Prometheus and visualize with Grafana

sub_nodes.title: 4.3 Logging, Debugging, and Troubleshooting Common Issues
sub_nodes.description: Theory: structured logging and log aggregation. Practice: instrument the agent with pino logger, ship logs to Loki, and create dashboards for error rate and latency spikes.
learning_points: Replace default console.log with pino logger for JSON output
Configure Fluentd or Vector to forward logs to Loki
Define alerting rules for API timeout and memory exhaustion

sub_nodes.title: 4.4 Scaling and High Availability with Container Orchestration
sub_nodes.description: Theory: microservices and orchestration patterns. Practice: deploy the agent as a Kubernetes Deployment with horizontal pod autoscaling, set up a load balancer, and test failover.
learning_points: Write Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, HPA)
Configure readiness and liveness probes for the agent
Simulate node failure and observe auto‑restart behavior

476.title: 1.2 Installing Prerequisites and Dependencies
476.description: Step‑by‑step installation of Node.js 22 via Homebrew, npm package manager, and optional Docker for containerized agents. Includes verification commands.
key_points: Use Homebrew to install Node.js 22 and link the binary
Verify version with node -v and npm -v
Install Docker if you plan to run agents in containers
476.detailed_content:
## Installing Prerequisites and Dependencies

### 1. Concept Explanation: Why This Matters

Setting up the correct development environment is the foundation of any successful AI agent project. Without proper prerequisites, you will encounter cryptic errors, version conflicts, and runtime failures that are difficult to debug. For Terminal AI Agents built with OpenClaw and OpenCode, Node.js serves as the runtime that executes the agent logic, while npm (Node Package Manager) handles all the library dependencies that power AI interactions, API calls, and terminal operations. Docker provides an isolated container environment where agents can run consistently across different systems, eliminating the classic "works on my machine" problem.

Understanding the installation process is crucial because version mismatches are the most common source of issues. OpenClaw and OpenCode specifically target Node.js 22 (LTS version codenamed 'Iron') because it offers improved performance, native TypeScript support, and enhanced ESM module handling that newer AI agent frameworks require.

### 2. Core Mechanics: How the Installation Works

**Homebrew as the Package Manager**

Homebrew is the de facto package manager for macOS and Linux. It simplifies software installation by handling dependency resolution, binary downloads, and PATH configuration automatically. When you install Node.js via Homebrew, it places the node and npm executables in `/usr/local/bin` (Intel Macs) or `/opt/homebrew/bin` (Apple Silicon), making them globally accessible from any terminal session.

**Node.js 22 and npm Integration**

Node.js 22 includes npm 10 by default, which means you get both tools in a single installation. The node runtime executes JavaScript and TypeScript code, while npm manages the package ecosystem—downloading, installing, updating, and removing project dependencies as defined in the `package.json` file.

**Docker Containerization**

Docker creates lightweight, portable containers that encapsulate the agent environment. When you run an agent inside a Docker container, it has its own filesystem, networking, and process space, isolated from the host system. This ensures that the agent runs identically whether you're on a developer's laptop, a CI/CD server, or production infrastructure.

### 3. Verification Process

After installation, verification commands confirm that everything is properly configured. Running `node -v` displays the installed Node.js version, while `npm -v` shows the npm version. For Docker, `docker --version` and `docker ps` confirm the daemon is running and containers can be spawned.

### 4. System Requirements and Considerations

- **macOS**: Ensure you have macOS 12 (Monterey) or later for full compatibility
- **Linux**: Ubuntu 20.04+, Debian 11+, or equivalent distributions
- **Windows**: Use WSL2 (Windows Subsystem for Linux) for best results
- **Hardware**: Minimum 4GB RAM recommended; 8GB preferred for running multiple agents

### 5. Installation Sequence

The recommended installation order is: first install Homebrew (if not present), then Node.js 22, then Docker. This sequence ensures that each component is available when needed by subsequent installations. Skipping verification steps after each installation allows you to catch problems early rather than debugging cascading failures.

### 6. Post-Installation Configuration

After installing Node.js, you may want to configure npm's global directory and prefix to avoid permission issues. Setting up a `.npmrc` file in your home directory with `prefix=~/.npm-global` routes global package installations to your user directory, eliminating the need for `sudo` when installing global packages.


## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
# Install Homebrew (if not already installed)
# This is the foundation package manager for macOS/Linux
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

# After installation, add Homebrew to your PATH (macOS Intel)
echo 'eval "$(/usr/local/bin/brew shellenv)"' >> ~/.zprofile
eval "$(/usr/local/bin/brew shellenv)"

# Install Node.js 22 using Homebrew
# The --greedy flag ensures we get the latest minor version
brew install node@22

# Link the Node.js installation to make it accessible globally
# This creates symlinks in /usr/local/bin or /opt/homebrew/bin
brew link node@22 --force

# Verify Node.js and npm installation
node -v    # Should output: v22.x.x
npm -v     # Should output: 10.x.x

# Configure npm to use a user-writable global directory
mkdir -p ~/.npm-global
npm config set prefix '~/.npm-global'
export PATH=~/.npm-global/bin:$PATH

# Add the PATH configuration to your shell profile for persistence
echo 'export PATH=~/.npm-global/bin:$PATH' >> ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc
```

### Example 2

```python
# Install Docker Desktop (macOS/Windows) or Docker Engine (Linux)
# macOS: Download Docker Desktop from https://docker.com/products/docker-desktop
# Linux: Use the official Docker installation script
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com -o get-docker.sh
sudo sh get-docker.sh

# Add your user to the docker group to avoid using sudo
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER

# Verify Docker installation
docker --version          # Should output: Docker version 24.x.x
docker info               # Shows Docker daemon status and configuration
docker ps                 # Lists running containers (should show header only initially)

# Test Docker with a simple container
docker run hello-world

# Pull a Node.js 22 base image for containerized agents
docker pull node:22-alpine

# Run an interactive Node.js session inside a container
docker run -it --rm node:22-alpine node -v

# Create a Dockerfile for your AI agent (example structure)
# FROM node:22-alpine
# WORKDIR /app
# COPY package*.json ./
# RUN npm install
# COPY . .
# CMD ["node", "agent.js"]

# Build and run your agent container
# docker build -t my-ai-agent .
# docker run -it --rm my-ai-agent
```



## Common Mistakes

- Skipping the verification step after Node.js installation, leading to 'command not found' errors when running node or npm
- Not linking Node.js 22 after Homebrew installation, causing the system to use an older pre-installed Node.js version
- Installing Node.js from nodejs.org instead of Homebrew, which can cause PATH conflicts and make version management difficult
- Forgetting to restart the terminal after installing Homebrew or linking Node.js, so the shell doesn't recognize the new commands
- Running Docker commands without proper permissions, resulting in 'permission denied' or 'cannot connect to Docker daemon' errors
- Not adding users to the docker group, requiring sudo for every Docker command and causing security concerns
- Installing an incompatible Node.js version (like 18 or 20) when the project specifically requires Node.js 22 features
- Ignoring Docker daemon status before trying to run containers, causing confusing error messages


## Best Practices

- Always verify installations immediately after completing them using version check commands (node -v, npm -v, docker --version)
- Use Homebrew exclusively for Node.js installation on macOS/Linux to ensure clean version management and easy updates with 'brew upgrade node@22'
- Create a .npmrc file with proper prefix configuration to avoid permission issues with global package installations
- Enable Docker Desktop/daemon to start on system boot for consistent development experience
- Pin specific Node.js and npm versions in your project using package.json's 'engines' field for team consistency
- Use Docker Compose for multi-container agent setups to manage complex dependencies and networking between containers
- Regularly update Homebrew itself with 'brew update' before installing packages to ensure you get the latest versions
- Document your complete environment setup in a README.md file so team members can reproduce the exact same configuration

478.title: 1.4 Quick‑Start Demo: Your First Terminal Agent
478.description: Create a simple skill that echoes user input, deploy it, and interact via terminal. Emphasizes theory‑practice loop: plan → code → test → iterate.
key_points: Define a basic skill manifest (name, trigger, actions)
Write the skill logic in JavaScript/TypeScript
Test with curl or telnet to the local agent port
code_examples:
#!/bin/bash
# test-agent.sh - Testing script using curl
# This demonstrates how to interact with the agent from the command line

AGENT_URL="http://localhost:3000"

echo "=== Testing Echo Skill ==="

# Test 1: Basic echo
curl -X POST "$AGENT_URL/execute" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"input": "echo Hello, World!"}' \
  -w "\n\nHTTP Status: %{http_code}\n"

echo ""

# Test 2: Echo with special characters
echo "=== Testing with Special Characters ==="
curl -X POST "$AGENT_URL/execute" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"input": "say Testing $100 and 100% success"}' \
  -w "\n\nHTTP Status: %{http_code}\n"

echo ""

# Test 3: Check agent status
echo "=== Agent Health Check ==="
curl -X GET "$AGENT_URL/health" \
  -w "\n\nHTTP Status: %{http_code}\n"

echo ""

# Test 4: List available skills
echo "=== Available Skills ==="
curl -X GET "$AGENT_URL/skills" \
  -w "\n\nHTTP Status: %{http_code}\n"

Launch agent with PM2 or systemd for background operation
477.detailed_content:
# Deploying OpenClaw on Local and Cloud Platforms

## Concept Explanation

Deploying OpenClaw as a terminal AI agent requires more than just running a script—it demands a production-grade setup that ensures reliability, security, and continuous operation. Whether you're running the agent locally for development or deploying it to Alibaba Cloud for production use, understanding the deployment lifecycle is essential.

### Why Deployment Configuration Matters

A poorly configured deployment can lead to API key exposure, unexpected crashes, inability to restart after system reboots, and resource contention with other services. OpenClaw acts as an autonomous agent that executes commands on your system; therefore, its deployment must be robust and secure.

### The Deployment Architecture

OpenClaw follows a client-server model where the agent process connects to model providers via API. The deployment process involves three critical layers:

1. **Runtime Environment** - Node.js runtime with all dependencies
2. **Configuration Layer** - Environment variables and credentials
3. **Process Management** - How the agent survives system events

## Core Mechanics

### Environment Variable Configuration

OpenClaw reads configuration from a `.env` file in the project root. These variables control authentication, model selection, and runtime behavior. Never hardcode sensitive values directly in scripts or commit them to version control.

### Process Supervision

Running OpenClaw directly with `node` or `npm start` creates a process tied to your terminal session—closing the terminal terminates the agent. Production deployments require a process supervisor that:

- Detaches from the terminal (daemon mode)
- Automatically restarts crashed processes
- Handles system startup and shutdown events
- Provides logging and monitoring

PM2 is ideal for development and staging environments, while systemd provides enterprise-grade reliability on Linux systems.

### Cloud Deployment Considerations

Alibaba Cloud deployment introduces network configuration, security group rules, and resource provisioning. The agent must be accessible via API while the underlying infrastructure remains secure.

## Runnable Code Examples

cd /opt/openclaw

echo "=== Installing Dependencies ==="

echo "=== Creating Environment Configuration ==="

OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-your-key-here
MODEL_PROVIDER=openai

# Agent Behavior

TEMPERATURE=0.7

// src/config/validator.js - Environment Validation
const requiredVars = [
  'OPENAI_API_KEY',
  'MODEL_PROVIDER',
  'MODEL_NAME'
];

const optionalVars = [
  'MAX_TOKENS',
  'TEMPERATURE',
  'AGENT_MODE',
  'LOG_LEVEL'
];

function validateEnvironment() {
  const missing = requiredVars.filter(v => !process.env[v]);
  
  if (missing.length > 0) {
    console.error('Missing required environment variables:');
    missing.forEach(v => console.error(`  - ${v}`));
    process.exit(1);
  }
  
  console.log('✓ Environment validation passed');
  console.log(`  Provider: ${process.env.MODEL_PROVIDER}`);
  console.log(`  Model: ${process.env.MODEL_NAME}`);
}

module.exports = { validateEnvironment };


## Common Mistakes and Gotchas

### 1. Exposing API Keys in Version Control

**Mistake**: Committing `.env` file or hardcoded credentials to git.

**Solution**: Always use `.gitignore` and consider a secrets manager for production.

bash
echo ".env" >> .gitignore
echo ".env.local" >> .gitignore
echo "node_modules/" >> .gitignore


### 2. Incorrect File Permissions

**Mistake**: Running with root or setting overly permissive permissions on `.env`.

**Consequence**: Security scanners may flag the repository, and some deployment tools reject insecure configurations.

**Fix**: 
bash
chmod 600 .env  # Owner read/write only
chmod 755 /opt/openclaw  # Executable for others


### 3. PM2 vs Systemd Confusion

**Mistake**: Running both PM2 and systemd for the same service, causing port conflicts and resource contention.

**Rule**: Choose one. PM2 for development flexibility, systemd for production reliability.

### 4. Ignoring Memory Limits

**Mistake**: Not setting `max_memory` in PM2 or `MemoryMax` in systemd.

**Consequence**: Agent may consume excessive memory during long operations or when processing large outputs.

### 5. Missing Dependency Installation

**Mistake**: Running `npm install` without `--production` flag, installing dev dependencies on production servers.

**Fix**: For production, use `--production` to reduce image size and attack surface.

### 6. Firewall Configuration on Cloud

**Mistake**: Deploying to Alibaba Cloud without configuring security groups.

**Impact**: Agent is unreachable, connection timeouts, apparent "crashes" that are actually network issues.

## Best Practices

### 1. Use Environment-Specific Configuration

Separate configurations for development, staging, and production. Use environment variable inheritance:

bash
# .env.production
NODE_ENV=production
LOG_LEVEL=warn
DEBUG_MODE=false
READ_ONLY_MODE=true


### 2. Implement Health Checks

Configure health check endpoints and restart policies:

javascript
// Add to src/index.js
const http = require('http');
const healthServer = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (req.url === '/health') {
    res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' });
    res.end(JSON.stringify({ status: 'healthy', uptime: process.uptime() }));
  }
});
healthServer.listen(3001);


### 3. Log Rotation Strategy

Configure log rotation to prevent disk space exhaustion:

bash
# /etc/logrotate.d/openclaw
/var/log/openclaw/*.log {
    daily
    rotate 14
    compress
    delaycompress
    missingok
    notifempty
    create 0640 $(whoami) $(id -gn)
}


### 4. Automated Backup of Configuration

bash
#!/bin/bash
# backup-config.sh - Run via cron daily
BACKUP_DIR="/opt/backups/openclaw"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)

mkdir -p $BACKUP_DIR
cp /opt/openclaw/.env $BACKUP_DIR/.env.$TIMESTAMP
cp /opt/openclaw/ecosystem.config.js $BACKUP_DIR/
find $BACKUP_DIR -name "*.env.*" -mtime +30 -delete


### 5. Monitoring Integration

Send metrics to monitoring systems:

javascript
// metrics.js - Prometheus-compatible metrics
export const metrics = {
  requests_total: 0,
  errors_total: 0,
  response_time_ms: []
};

function recordMetric(name, value) {
  console.log(`# HELP openclaw_${name} OpenClaw agent metric`);
  console.log(`# TYPE openclaw_${name} gauge`);
  console.log(`openclaw_${name} ${value}`);
}


### 6. Graceful Shutdown Handling

Always handle SIGTERM for clean container/systemd shutdowns:

javascript
process.on('SIGTERM', async () => {
  console.log('Received SIGTERM, shutting down gracefully...');
  await agent.saveState();
  await db.closeConnections();
  process.exit(0);
});

process.on('SIGINT', async () => {
  console.log('Received SIGINT, initiating shutdown...');
  process.exit(0);
});


### 7. Security Hardening Checklist

- Rotate API keys regularly (every 90 days)
- Use VPC/private networking on cloud platforms
- Implement rate limiting on agent endpoints
- Audit command execution logs
- Restrict shell access to necessary users only

## Common Mistakes

- Committing .env file to git repository, exposing API keys and credentials publicly
- Running both PM2 and systemd for the same service, causing port conflicts and resource contention
- Setting incorrect file permissions (777) on .env file instead of restrictive 600
- Not configuring log rotation, leading to disk space exhaustion on long-running deployments
- Skipping health check endpoints, making it impossible to detect agent failures in monitoring systems
- Deploying without configuring firewall/security groups on cloud platforms, causing apparent 'crashes'
- Using development API keys in production environment, risking rate limits and security issues
- Not setting memory limits, causing agent to consume excessive resources during large operations
- Running npm install without --production flag on production servers, installing unnecessary dev dependencies
- Failing to handle SIGTERM signals properly, resulting in lost state and corrupted configuration files
- Using absolute paths that only work on local machine instead of relative paths for portability
- Not validating environment variables at startup, leading to cryptic errors later during execution


## Best Practices

- Use separate .env files for development, staging, and production with inheritance pattern
- Implement environment validation at startup to fail fast with clear error messages
- Configure health check endpoints (/health) for monitoring and load balancer integration
- Set up log rotation using logrotate or PM2's built-in log rotation feature
- Use systemd for production deployments and PM2 for development/staging
- Implement graceful shutdown handlers for SIGTERM and SIGINT to save state properly
- Automate configuration backups to a secure location via cron jobs
- Rotate API keys every 90 days and use environment-specific credentials
- Set resource limits (memory, CPU) in process manager configuration
- Use private networking/VPC on cloud platforms and restrict firewall rules to minimum necessary
- Create automated startup validation script to verify all dependencies and configurations
- Implement proper logging with structured output (JSON) for easy parsing by log aggregation tools
- Use process supervisor's built-in restart policies instead of custom wrapper scripts
- Store sensitive configuration in secrets manager (Alibaba Cloud KMS, HashiCorp Vault) for production
- Monitor agent health metrics and set up alerts for unusual patterns like high error rates or resource spikes

479.title: 2.1 Shaping Agent Personality with Config Files
479.description: Theory: explore the schema of OpenClaw’s config (personality traits, response style). Practice: edit config.yaml to adjust tone, verbosity, and skill activation rules.

479.detailed_content:
## Understanding Agent Personality Configuration

When you interact with an AI agent in a terminal environment, the agent's behavior doesn't emerge from magic — it is shaped by a structured configuration file (typically `config.yaml` or `config.json`) that defines how the agent thinks, speaks, decides, and acts. Understanding how to craft this configuration is the difference between a generic chatbot and a purpose-built terminal assistant that feels like an expert collaborator.

### Why Configuration-Driven Personality Matters

An AI agent without personality configuration responds with uniform, one-size-fits-all behavior. In a development workflow, this is insufficient because:

- **Different tasks demand different tones** — debugging requires terse, diagnostic communication; brainstorming requires verbose, exploratory dialogue.
- **Tool usage must be contextual** — an agent shouldn't activate file system tools during a conversation about API design unless explicitly appropriate.
- **Memory and recall behavior affects productivity** — some workflows benefit from aggressive context compression, others need verbose memory retention.

The config file acts as the agent's "persona layer" — a declarative specification that influences every response without requiring you to rewrite prompts manually for each session.

---

## Core Mechanics: The Config Schema

OpenClaw's configuration schema typically organizes into three primary sections:

### 1. Personality Section
This section controls how the agent communicates. It defines:

- **Tone** — formal, casual, technical, empathetic, etc.
- **Verbosity** — brief, moderate, verbose, exhaustive
- **Response style** — structured (with headings, lists) vs. natural prose
- **Domain expertise level** — novice, intermediate, expert (affects explanation depth)

yaml
personality:
  tone: technical
  verbosity: moderate
  response_style: structured
  expertise: intermediate
  traits:
    - methodical
    - security-conscious
    - documentation-oriented


### 2. Toolset Section
This section defines which tools are available and under what conditions they activate. It supports:

- **Conditional activation** — tools activate only when specific conditions are met
- **Permission levels** — read-only vs. write-permitted tools
- **Timeout and retry policies** per tool

yaml
toolset:
  enabled:
    - file_read
    - shell_execute
    - git_query
    - web_search
  disabled: []
  activation_rules:
    file_write:
      requires_confirmation: true
      max_file_size_kb: 512
    shell_execute:
      allowed_commands:
        - grep
        - find
        - cat
        - ls
      denied_patterns:
        - rm -rf /
        - format
  tool_timeout_seconds: 30


### 3. Memory Section
Controls how the agent retains and uses conversation history and context:

- **Context window** — how many recent messages are kept in working memory
- **Compression strategy** — whether to summarize older context or drop it
- **Persistence** — whether memory survives between sessions

yaml
memory:
  context_window_messages: 20
  compression:
    enabled: true
    strategy: semantic_summarize
    threshold_messages: 15
  persistence:
    enabled: true
    storage_path: ./memory_store
    recall_weight: recent


### JSON Schema Validation

Before loading your config, OpenClaw validates it against a JSON schema. This catches structural errors early — missing required fields, wrong types, out-of-range values — before the agent even starts. If your config fails validation, the agent typically refuses to start and displays a detailed error report.

Schema validation is crucial because a malformed config can cause silent failures where the agent defaults to bare-bones behavior without obvious error messages.

---

## Testing Personality Changes Live

The most effective way to understand config impact is to test changes in a live chat loop. The typical workflow:

1. Start the agent with a known config
2. Ask a test question that exercises multiple personality aspects
3. Modify the config
4. Restart the agent (or hot-reload if supported)
5. Ask the same question again and compare responses

This iterative approach reveals how small config changes ripple through the agent's behavior.

---

## Key Configuration Patterns

### Pattern 1: Task-Specific Persona Override
Define multiple persona profiles in the config and switch between them programmatically:

yaml
personas:
  default:
    tone: technical
    verbosity: moderate
  debugging:
    tone: terse
    verbosity: minimal
    focus_tools: [shell_execute, log_read]
  architectural_review:
    tone: formal
    verbosity: verbose
    traits: [thorough, design-conscious]


### Pattern 2: Tool Safety Gates
Implement progressive permission escalation based on task context:

yaml
toolset:
  file_write:
    auto_deny_patterns:
      - "*.env"
      - "*credentials*"
      - "*/.ssh/*"
    require_explicit_approval: true
    audit_log: true


### Pattern 3: Dynamic Memory Tuning
Adjust memory behavior based on session length:

yaml
memory:
  dynamic_sizing: true
  short_session_threshold: 5
  long_session_threshold: 50
  short_session_compression: false
  long_session_aggressive_compression: true



## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
import yaml
import json

# Simulating OpenClaw config validation and loading
def load_and_validate_config(config_path: str) -> dict:
    """
    Load YAML config and validate against JSON schema.
    In real OpenClaw, this uses jsonschema library.
    """
    with open(config_path, 'r') as f:
        config = yaml.safe_load(f)
    
    # Validate required sections exist
    required_sections = ['personality', 'toolset', 'memory']
    missing = [s for s in required_sections if s not in config]
    
    if missing:
        raise ValueError(f"Missing required sections: {missing}")
    
    # Validate personality subsections
    personality_required = ['tone', 'verbosity', 'response_style']
    for field in personality_required:
        if field not in config['personality']:
            raise ValueError(f"Missing personality field: {field}")
    
    print(f"[CONFIG] Loaded and validated successfully")
    print(f"[CONFIG] Tone: {config['personality']['tone']}")
    print(f"[CONFIG] Tools enabled: {config['toolset']['enabled']}")
    print(f"[CONFIG] Context window: {config['memory']['context_window_messages']} messages")
    
    return config


# Example config file content
config_yaml = '''
personality:
  tone: technical
  verbosity: moderate
  response_style: structured
  expertise: intermediate
  traits:
    - methodical
    - security-conscious

toolset:
  enabled:
    - file_read
    - shell_execute
    - git_query
  disabled: []
  activation_rules:
    shell_execute:
      allowed_commands:
        - grep
        - find
        - cat
      denied_patterns:
        - "rm -rf /"
  tool_timeout_seconds: 30

memory:
  context_window_messages: 20
  compression:
    enabled: true
    strategy: semantic_summarize
    threshold_messages: 15
  persistence:
    enabled: true
'''

# Write temp config and load it
import tempfile
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', suffix='.yaml', delete=False) as f:
    f.write(config_yaml)
    temp_path = f.name

config = load_and_validate_config(temp_path)
print(f"\n[SUCCESS] Config loaded from: {temp_path}")
```

### Example 2

```python
# Simulating a personality change and testing with a chat loop
class AgentChatLoop:
    def __init__(self, config: dict):
        self.config = config
        self.messages = []
    
    def generate_response(self, user_input: str) -> str:
        """
        Simulates how config influences agent response.
        In real OpenClaw, this uses LLM API with system prompt injection.
        """
        tone = self.config['personality']['tone']
        verbosity = self.config['personality']['verbosity']
        style = self.config['personality']['response_style']
        traits = self.config['personality'].get('traits', [])
        
        # Simulate response based on personality settings
        if tone == 'technical' and verbosity == 'moderate':
            response = f"[Technical Analysis] Regarding '{user_input}': "
            response += "Analyzing with traits: " + ", ".join(traits)
        elif tone == 'terse' and verbosity == 'minimal':
            response = f"Result for '{user_input}': Done."
        else:
            response = f"Processing '{user_input}' with default settings."
        
        if style == 'structured':
            response = f"## Response\n{response}\n- Config tone: {tone}\n- Verbosity: {verbosity}"
        
        return response
    
    def update_personality(self, new_tone: str, new_verbosity: str):
        """Hot-reload personality config without full restart."""
        self.config['personality']['tone'] = new_tone
        self.config['personality']['verbosity'] = new_verbosity
        print(f"[CONFIG] Personality updated: tone={new_tone}, verbosity={new_verbosity}")


# Test the personality change workflow
test_config = {
    'personality': {
        'tone': 'technical',
        'verbosity': 'moderate',
        'response_style': 'structured',
        'traits': ['methodical', 'security-conscious']
    },
    'toolset': {'enabled': ['file_read', 'shell_execute']},
    'memory': {'context_window_messages': 20}
}

agent = AgentChatLoop(test_config)

print("=== Initial Configuration ===")
print(f"Question: 'How do I remove a git commit?'")
print(f"Agent: {agent.generate_response('How do I remove a git commit?')}")

print("\n=== After Personality Change ===")
agent.update_personality('terse', 'minimal')
print(f"Question: 'How do I remove a git commit?'")
print(f"Agent: {agent.generate_response('How do I remove a git commit?')}")
```



## Common Mistakes

- Omitting required schema fields causes the agent to silently fall back to defaults — always validate your config against the JSON schema before deployment
- Setting verbosity to 'verbose' without adjusting context window can cause token overflow in long conversations — these two settings must be coordinated
- Enabling all tools by default creates security vulnerabilities — always implement activation rules even for trusted environments
- Forgetting to update the config schema version when migrating between OpenClaw versions causes validation failures or runtime errors
- Hot-reloading config without restarting the tool session causes stale memory state — if memory persistence is enabled, clear the memory store after major personality changes


## Best Practices

- Use persona profiles instead of monolithic single-config — define multiple named personas (debugging, architectural, exploratory) and switch contextually for better task alignment
- Always run JSON schema validation in your CI pipeline before pushing config changes — treat config files like production code
- Test personality changes in isolation — modify one field at a time and record the before/after behavior to understand each knob's effect
- Implement tool deny-lists as a safety net even if you trust the LLM — explicit deny patterns catch accidental dangerous commands the model might otherwise execute
- Log all config changes with a timestamp and reason — this creates an audit trail that helps you reproduce and debug unexpected agent behavior weeks later
- Keep config files version-controlled and reviewable — avoid hardcoding sensitive values in the config; use environment variable references instead
- Set conservative tool timeouts initially and increase them only for specific long-running commands — a runaway shell command can consume resources indefinitely without proper timeout enforcement

480.title: 2.2 Long‑Term Memory with File Store and Vector DB
480.description: Theory: discuss memory architectures (key‑value, embeddings). Practice: enable the built‑in file store and optionally add a Pinecone or FAISS vector store for semantic recall.
key_points: Configure memory provider in config.yaml
Generate embeddings for user‑provided documents
Query memory from a skill to retrieve past context
480.detailed_content:
# Long-Term Memory with File Store and Vector DB

## 1. Concept Explanation: Why Long-Term Memory Matters

In terminal AI agents, short-term context windows are limited. Without persistent memory, an agent forgets everything after each conversation turn or task completion. Long-term memory solves this by storing:

- **User preferences and patterns** - Learning how a developer prefers to work
- **Project-specific knowledge** - Understanding codebase conventions, naming standards, and architectural decisions
- **Historical interactions** - Remembering past solutions, failed approaches, and successful strategies
- **Document context** - Maintaining awareness of referenced documents and their key information

Memory transforms a stateless agent into a learning system that improves over time with each interaction.

## 2. Core Mechanics: How Memory Architectures Work

### 2.1 Key-Value Store (File Store)

The file store operates as a simple key-value persistence layer:


user_preferences: {'theme': 'dark', 'language': 'python'}
project_config: {'root': '/workspace', 'env': 'dev'}
skill_context: {'last_task': 'api_integration', 'status': 'completed'}


**How it works:**
1. Data is serialized (JSON, pickle, or binary format)
2. Stored in a designated directory on the filesystem
3. Retrieved by exact key match or prefix scanning
4. No semantic understanding—purely structural retrieval

**Use cases:** Configuration persistence, session state, quick lookups where exact matching suffices.

### 2.2 Embeddings and Vector Store

For semantic (meaning-based) recall, we use embedding vectors:


Document: "The API uses OAuth2 with JWT tokens for authentication"
Embedding: [0.123, -0.456, 0.789, ...]  // 1536-dimensional vector


**How it works:**
1. Text is processed through an embedding model (OpenAI ada-002, sentence-transformers, etc.)
2. Each document becomes a point in high-dimensional space
3. Similar concepts cluster together (semantic proximity)
4. Queries are converted to vectors and matched against stored vectors
5. Cosine similarity or dot product identifies closest matches

**Use cases:** Finding relevant documentation, context-aware suggestions, semantic search across project knowledge.

### 2.3 Hybrid Architecture

OpenClaw implements a layered memory system:


Query Request
     │
     ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│   Intent Check  │  ──► Is this a factual recall? Use Key-Value
└────────┬────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Semantic Search │  ──► Is this meaning-based? Use Vector DB
└────────┬────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│  Memory Fusion  │  ──► Combine results, rank by relevance
└─────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
   Retrieved Context


## 3. Configuration Deep Dive

### config.yaml Structure

yaml
memory:
  enabled: true
  providers:
    file_store:
      enabled: true
      path: "./memory/store"
      max_size_mb: 512
    vector_store:
      enabled: true
      provider: "pinecone"  # or "faiss"
      dimension: 1536
      metric: "cosine"


### Provider-Specific Configuration

**Pinecone (Cloud):**
yaml
vector_store:
  provider: "pinecone"
  api_key: "${PINECONE_API_KEY}"  # Environment variable
  environment: "us-west1-gcp"
  index_name: "openclaw-memory"


**FAISS (Local):**
yaml
vector_store:
  provider: "faiss"
  index_path: "./memory/vectors/index.faiss"
  dimension: 1536


## 4. Memory Operations Flow

### Storing Information

1. **Identify worthiness** - Is this information worth remembering?
2. **Chunk strategically** - Break large documents into searchable units (512-1024 tokens)
3. **Generate embedding** - Convert each chunk to a vector
4. **Store dual-index** - Save to both file store (key-value) and vector store (semantic)

### Retrieval Process

1. **Query embedding** - Convert user query to vector representation
2. **Vector search** - Find top-k semantically similar chunks
3. **Score filtering** - Discard results below relevance threshold (0.7)
4. **Re-ranking** - Combine with metadata (recency, frequency, importance)
5. **Context injection** - Prepend retrieved context to agent prompt

## 5. Integration with Skills

Skills access memory through a standardized interface:

python
# Skill requests memory context
context = await agent.memory.query(
    query="authentication implementation patterns",
    limit=5,
    filters={'source': 'codebase', 'language': 'python'}
)


Memory context appears in the system prompt, giving the agent historical awareness without manual context management.


## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
# Example 1: File Store Configuration and Basic Operations
# Location: config.yaml

memory:
  enabled: true
  providers:
    file_store:
      enabled: true
      path: "./memory/store"
      max_size_mb: 512
      eviction_policy: "lru"  # least recently used
    vector_store:
      enabled: true
      provider: "faiss"  # local fallback
      dimension: 1536
      metric: "cosine"

---

# Example 1b: Python integration with file store

from openclaw import Agent, MemoryStore

# Initialize agent with memory enabled
agent = Agent(
    name="DevAssistant",
    memory_path="./memory/store"
)

# Storing structured data (key-value)
await agent.memory.store(
    key="user:preferences",
    value={
        "code_style": "pep8",
        "test_framework": "pytest",
        "documentation": "sphinx"
    }
)

# Retrieve by exact key
preferences = await agent.memory.get("user:preferences")

# Store document chunks for semantic search
document_chunks = [
    {"id": "doc1-chunk1", "text": "OAuth2 implementation uses JWT tokens...", "source": "auth_readme.md"},
    {"id": "doc1-chunk2", "text": "Token refresh happens every 30 minutes...", "source": "auth_readme.md"},
]

await agent.memory.store_embeddings(document_chunks)

# Query memory semantically
results = await agent.memory.query(
    query="How does token authentication work?",
    limit=3,
    min_score=0.75  # only high-confidence matches
)

for result in results:
    print(f"[{result.score:.2f}] {result.text}")
```

### Example 2

```python
# Example 2: Vector Store with Pinecone Integration

import os
from openclaw import Agent, VectorMemory

# Configure with environment variable for security
os.environ["PINECONE_API_KEY"] = os.getenv("PINECONE_API_KEY")

# Initialize vector store with provider settings
vector_store = VectorMemory(
    provider="pinecone",
    environment="us-west1-gcp",
    index_name="project-memory",
    dimension=1536,
    metadata_fields=["source", "project", "timestamp", "type"]
)

# Batch upload documents with metadata
documents = [
    {
        "text": "Project uses async PostgreSQL with SQLAlchemy 2.0",
        "metadata": {"source": "architecture.md", "project": "backend-api", "type": "architecture"}
    },
    {
        "text": "Database connection pooling: min=5, max=20",
        "metadata": {"source": "config.py", "project": "backend-api", "type": "config"}
    },
    {
        "text": "Migrations handled via Alembic, run on deployment",
        "metadata": {"source": "README.md", "project": "backend-api", "type": "process"}
    }
]

# Store and get embeddings generated automatically
await vector_store.add_documents(documents)

# Semantic query with metadata filtering
results = await vector_store.search(
    query="database connection settings",
    filter={"project": "backend-api"},  # restrict to specific project
    top_k=5
)

# FAISS local alternative
faiss_store = VectorMemory(
    provider="faiss",
    index_path="./memory/vectors/index.faiss",
    dimension=1536
)

# Persist and load FAISS index
await faiss_store.save_index()
faiss_store.load_index()
```

### Example 3

```python
# Example 3: Skill Accessing Memory for Context

from openclaw import skill, Agent

@skill(name="implement_endpoint", description="Create REST API endpoints")
async def implement_endpoint(agent: Agent, request: dict):
    """
    Skill that retrieves similar past implementations for reference.
    """
    # Query memory for similar past tasks
    similar_tasks = await agent.memory.query(
        query=f"API endpoint implementation {request.get('method', 'GET')} {request.get('path', '/')}",
        limit=5,
        filters={"type": "implementation", "status": "completed"}
    )
    
    # Build context from retrieved memory
    context_parts = []
    if similar_tasks:
        context_parts.append("## Similar Past Implementations:\n")
        for task in similar_tasks:
            context_parts.append(f"- {task.text}")
    
    # Get project conventions from file store
    conventions = await agent.memory.get(f"project:{agent.project_id}:conventions")
    if conventions:
        context_parts.append(f"\n## Project Conventions: {conventions}")
    
    context = "\n".join(context_parts) if context_parts else "No prior context found."
    
    # Inject into the skill's reasoning
    prompt = f"""
    Context from memory:
    {context}
    
    Task: {request}
    
    Implement the endpoint following the patterns above.
    """
    
    return await agent.reason(prompt)
```



## Common Mistakes

- Storing everything without filtering - leads to bloated memory with low signal-to-noise ratio; implement relevance scoring before storing
- Ignoring chunk size optimization - chunks that are too large lose granularity; too small lose context; aim for 512-1024 tokens with 50-token overlap
- Not configuring TTL/expiry for transient data - stale preferences and outdated context pollute memory; set appropriate expiration policies
- Mixing security-sensitive data without encryption - API keys, tokens, or credentials stored in memory can be exposed; use encrypted stores or avoid storing secrets
- Forgetting to rebuild vector index after updates - FAISS indexes become stale when documents are added or deleted; schedule periodic rebuilds
- Setting similarity threshold too low (below 0.6) - returns irrelevant matches that confuse the agent; 0.7-0.8 is typically safer
- Using single embedding model for heterogeneous content - code, prose, and configs have different semantic patterns; consider domain-specific embeddings


## Best Practices

- Implement a memory triage system that evaluates new information for uniqueness, relevance, and recency before storing - not everything deserves permanent memory
- Use hierarchical chunking: store both document-level summaries and paragraph-level details for flexible retrieval at different granularities
- Tag all stored content with source, timestamp, and importance score to enable intelligent filtering during retrieval
- Configure separate memory namespaces per project/user to prevent cross-contamination and enable targeted cleanup
- Schedule periodic memory audits: review stored content, remove outdated information, and refine embedding quality
- Implement memory versioning to track how knowledge evolves over time - especially useful for project-specific documentation
- Expose memory usage metrics (store size, query latency, hit rate) to monitor performance and optimize resource allocation
- Use hybrid queries: combine semantic search with keyword matching for more precise results, especially for technical terminology

481.title: 2.3 Connecting Telegram and Email as Action Tools
481.description: Theory: examine OpenClaw’s tool interface for external APIs. Practice: register Telegram bot, set webhook, and write a skill that reads inbox and sends alerts.
key_points: Create a Telegram bot via BotFather and capture the token
Implement OAuth2 for Gmail API access
Write a skill that posts a daily summary to a chat room
481.detailed_content:
# Connecting Telegram and Email as Action Tools ## 1. Concept Explanation: Why This Matters In the ecosystem of Terminal AI Agents built with OpenClaw and OpenCode, the ability to interact with external services transforms a simple CLI tool into a powerful communication hub. Telegram and Email represent two fundamental communication channels that, when integrated as Action Tools, enable your AI agent to: - **Send proactive notifications** to users regardless of their location - **Receive commands and queries** through familiar messaging interfaces - **Bridge the gap between terminal automation and human interaction** - **Create alert systems** that monitor processes and report back in real-time ### The Strategic Advantage When your AI agent can post daily summaries to a Telegram chat room, it becomes a team member that never sleeps. Similarly, Email integration allows your agent to send formal notifications, reports, or alerts to stakeholders who prefer email communication over chat platforms. ## 2. Core Mechanics: How It Works ### Telegram Integration Architecture OpenClaw provides a standardized **Tool Interface** that abstracts the complexity of external API communication. When you connect Telegram, you interact with this interface through the following flow: [OpenClaw Agent] → [Tool Interface] → [Telegram Bot API] → [User's Telegram Chat] The process involves three critical steps: 1. **Bot Registration**: Create a bot through Telegram's BotFather to obtain a unique token 2. **Webhook Configuration**: Set up an endpoint where Telegram can forward incoming messages 3. **Skill Implementation**: Write code that uses the bot token to send/receive messages ### Email Integration via OAuth2 Gmail API access requires OAuth2 authentication, which follows this security model: [User] → [Authorization Request] → [Gmail API] → [Access Token] → [Agent Actions] OAuth2 ensures that your agent never stores actual user credentials, instead using scoped access tokens that can be revoked at any time. ## 3. The Tool Interface Pattern OpenClaw's tool interface follows a consistent pattern that makes adding new integrations predictable: - **Configuration Phase**: Register API credentials and endpoints - **Initialization Phase**: Establish connections and validate permissions - **Execution Phase**: Perform actions (send message, read inbox, etc.) - **Cleanup Phase**: Close connections and handle rate limits ## 4. Understanding Webhooks vs Polling For Telegram, you have two options: | Method | Pros | Cons | Best For | |--------|------|------|----------| | Webhooks | Real-time, efficient | Requires public URL | Production systems | | Polling | Simple setup | Resource-intensive | Development, private networks | For this knowledge point, we focus on the webhook approach as it's the recommended production pattern. ## 5. Security Considerations When integrating external services, security must be paramount: - **Never expose tokens** in version control (use environment variables) - **Implement token rotation** for long-running agents - **Validate incoming requests** to prevent spoofing attacks - **Scope permissions** to the minimum required (principle of least privilege) ## Code Examples ### Example 1 ```python # Example 1: Telegram Bot Setup and Daily Summary Skill # This example demonstrates complete Telegram integration with OpenClaw import os import json from datetime import datetime, timedelta from openclaw import Agent, ToolInterface, skill class TelegramTool(ToolInterface): """OpenClaw Tool Interface for Telegram Bot API integration.""" def __init__(self, bot_token: str, allowed_chat_ids: list = None): self.bot_token = bot_token self.allowed_chat_ids = allowed_chat_ids or [] self.base_url = f"https://api.telegram.org/bot{bot_token}" def configure(self, config: dict): """Configure the tool with provided settings.""" self.webhook_url = config.get("webhook_url") self.admin_chat_id = config.get("admin_chat_id") def initialize(self): """Initialize connection and set up webhook.""" # Set the webhook endpoint for receiving messages webhook_endpoint = f"{self.webhook_url}/webhook/telegram" response = self._make_request("setWebhook", { "url": webhook_endpoint, "allowed_updates": ["message", "callback_query"] }) return response.get("ok", False) def send_message(self, chat_id: int, text: str, parse_mode: str = "Markdown") -> dict: """Send a message to a specific Telegram chat.""" if chat_id not in self.allowed_chat_ids and chat_id != self.admin_chat_id: raise PermissionError(f"Chat ID {chat_id} not authorized") return self._make_request("sendMessage", { "chat_id": chat_id, "text": text, "parse_mode": parse_mode }) def _make_request(self, method: str, data: dict) -> dict: """Internal method to make API requests to Telegram.""" import requests url = f"{self.base_url}/{method}" response = requests.post(url, json=data, timeout=30) response.raise_for_status() return response.json() # Skill definition for posting daily summaries @skill(name="daily_summary", trigger="scheduled", schedule="0 9 * * *") async def post_daily_summary(agent: Agent, context: dict): """Post a daily summary to the configured chat room. This skill is triggered daily at 9 AM and compiles information from various sources to create a comprehensive daily report. """ telegram_tool = agent.get_tool("telegram") # Compile summary data from various sources summary_data = await gather_daily_metrics(context) # Format the message with emojis for visual appeal summary_text = f""" 📊 *Daily Summary - {datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}* 🔹 *System Status*: {summary_data['status']} 🔹 *Tasks Completed*: {summary_data['tasks_completed']} 🔹 *Errors Encountered*: {summary_data['errors']} 🔹 *Active Users*: {summary_data['active_users']} 📝 *Highlights*: {chr(10).join(f" • {item}" for item in summary_data['highlights'])} ⏰ *Next Scheduled Task*: {summary_data['next_task']} """ # Send to the configured admin chat await telegram_tool.send_message( chat_id=telegram_tool.admin_chat_id, text=summary_text.strip() ) async def gather_daily_metrics(context: dict) -> dict: """Gather metrics from various sources for the summary.""" # Placeholder - in production, this would query actual data sources return { "status": "🟢 Operational", "tasks_completed": 42, "errors": 0, "active_users": 15, "highlights": [ "Completed automated backup sequence", "Processed 1,247 incoming emails", "Generated weekly performance report" ], "next_task": "Weekly cleanup at 18:00" } # Example usage in agent initialization def setup_telegram_agent(): """Initialize an agent with Telegram integration.""" agent = Agent(name="DailyReporter") # Register the Telegram tool with credentials from environment telegram_tool = TelegramTool( bot_token=os.environ["TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN"], allowed_chat_ids=[int(x) for x in os.environ.get("ALLOWED_CHAT_IDS", "").split(",")] ) # Configure the tool telegram_tool.configure({ "webhook_url": os.environ["WEBHOOK_BASE_URL"], "admin_chat_id": int(os.environ["ADMIN_CHAT_ID"]) }) # Initialize and register the tool if telegram_tool.initialize(): agent.register_tool("telegram", telegram_tool) # Register the skill agent.register_skill(post_daily_summary) return agent ``` ### Example 2 ```python # Example 2: Gmail API OAuth2 Integration for Email Alerts # This example shows complete Gmail API setup with OAuth2 for email operations import os import json import base64 from email.mime.text import MIMEText from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart from google.oauth2.credentials import Credentials from google_auth_oauthlib.flow import InstalledAppFlow from google.auth.transport.requests import Request from openclaw import ToolInterface, skill # If modifying these scopes, delete the file token.json SCOPES = [ 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.read', 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.send', 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.modify' ] class GmailTool(ToolInterface): """OpenClaw Tool Interface for Gmail API with OAuth2 authentication.""" def __init__(self, credentials_path: str, token_path: str = "token.json"): self.credentials_path = credentials_path self.token_path = token_path self.creds = None self.service = None def configure(self, config: dict): """Configure additional Gmail tool settings.""" self.sender_email = config.get("sender_email") self.alert_recipients = config.get("alert_recipients", []) self.max_results = config.get("max_results", 10) def initialize(self) -> bool: """Initialize OAuth2 flow and establish Gmail API connection.""" self.creds = self._get_credentials() if not self.creds or not self.creds.valid: if self.creds and self.creds.expired and self.creds.refresh_token: self.creds.refresh(Request()) else: return False # Build the Gmail API service from googleapiclient.discovery import build self.service = build('gmail', 'v1', credentials=self.creds) return True def _get_credentials(self) -> Credentials: """Handle OAuth2 token storage and refresh.""" if os.path.exists(self.token_path): return Credentials.from_authorized_user_file(self.token_path, SCOPES) return None def _save_credentials(self): """Save the credentials token for future use.""" with open(self.token_path, 'w') as token: token.write(self.creds.to_json()) def create_message(self, to: str, subject: str, body: str, html_body: str = None) -> dict: """Create an email message in MIME format.""" message = MIMEMultipart('alternative') message['to'] = to message['from'] = self.sender_email message['subject'] = subject # Attach both plain text and HTML versions message.attach(MIMEText(body, 'plain')) if html_body: message.attach(MIMEText(html_body, 'html')) # Encode the message for API submission encoded_message = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(message.as_bytes()) return { 'raw': encoded_message.decode('utf-8') } def send_email(self, to: str, subject: str, body: str, html_body: str = None) -> dict: """Send an email message via Gmail API.""" if not self.service: raise RuntimeError("Gmail service not initialized") message = self.create_message(to, subject, body, html_body) try: sent_message = self.service.users().messages().send( userId='me', body=message ).execute() return { "success": True, "message_id": sent_message['id'], "thread_id": sent_message['threadId'] } except Exception as e: return { "success": False, "error": str(e) } def read_inbox(self, max_results: int = None) -> list: """Read recent emails from inbox.""" if not self.service: raise RuntimeError("Gmail service not initialized") max_results = max_results or self.max_results try: results = self.service.users().messages().list( userId='me', labelIds=['INBOX'], maxResults=max_results ).execute() messages = results.get('messages', []) email_details = [] for msg in messages: detail = self.service.users().messages().get( userId='me', id=msg['id'], format='metadata', metadataHeaders=['Subject', 'From', 'Date'] ).execute() email_details.append(detail) return email_details except Exception as e: print(f"Error reading inbox: {e}") return [] # OAuth2 authorization helper function def authorize_gmail(credentials_path: str, token_path: str = "token.json") -> Credentials: """Perform the OAuth2 authorization flow for Gmail API. This function initiates the browser-based OAuth consent flow. Run this once to generate the token.json file, then subsequent runs will use the cached token. """ flow = InstalledAppFlow.from_client_secrets_file(credentials_path, SCOPES) creds = flow.run_local_server(port=8080, prompt='consent') # Save credentials for future runs with open(token_path, 'w') as token: token.write(creds.to_json()) return creds # Skill for sending email alerts @skill(name="email_alert", trigger="event", event_type="system_error") async def send_error_alert(agent: Agent, event_data: dict): """Send an email alert when system errors occur. This skill is triggered by system_error events and sends formatted email alerts to configured recipients. """ gmail_tool = agent.get_tool("gmail") error_message = event_data.get("message", "Unknown error occurred") error_severity = event_data.get("severity", "MEDIUM") timestamp = event_data.get("timestamp", "N/A") # Format email content subject = f"⚠️ [{error_severity}] System Alert - {timestamp}" body = f""" System Alert Notification Severity: {error_severity} Timestamp: {timestamp} Error Message: {error_message} Please investigate the system immediately. This is an automated alert from your OpenClaw AI Agent. """ html_body = f"""

Severity {error_severity} Timestamp {timestamp} Error Message {error_message}

This is an automated alert from your OpenClaw AI Agent.

""" # Send to all alert recipients for recipient in gmail_tool.alert_recipients: result = gmail_tool.send_email( to=recipient, subject=subject, body=body, html_body=html_body ) print(f"Alert sent to {recipient}: {result['success']}") # Example initialization def setup_gmail_agent(): """Initialize an agent with Gmail integration.""" agent = Agent(name="EmailEnabledAgent") # Check if we need to authorize if not os.path.exists("token.json"): print("Performing OAuth2 authorization...") authorize_gmail("credentials.json") # Create and register the Gmail tool gmail_tool = GmailTool( credentials_path="credentials.json", token_path="token.json" ) gmail_tool.configure({ "sender_email": os.environ["SENDER_EMAIL"], "alert_recipients": os.environ["ALERT_RECIPIENTS"].split(","), "max_results": 20 }) if gmail_tool.initialize(): agent.register_tool("gmail", gmail_tool) # Register the email alert skill agent.register_skill(send_error_alert) return agent ``` ## Common Mistakes - Hardcoding API tokens and credentials directly in source code instead of using environment variables, making them visible in version control - Forgetting to set up webhook validation, allowing anyone to send fake messages that appear to come from Telegram users - Not implementing proper error handling for API rate limits, causing the agent to crash when Telegram's rate limits are exceeded - Using the same bot token for multiple instances without proper rate limit management - Neglecting to refresh OAuth2 tokens before they expire, resulting in sudden authentication failures during production operation - Setting overly broad OAuth2 scopes (like full Gmail access) when only specific operations are needed, violating the principle of least privilege - Not properly validating incoming webhook payloads, which could allow malicious data to be processed by the agent - Missing the async/await implementation in skills that interact with external APIs, leading to blocking operations and poor performance - Forgetting to save the refreshed OAuth2 credentials back to storage, causing the refresh token to be used only once - Not handling the case where the Telegram bot is added to a group without proper privacy mode configuration ## Best Practices - Always store sensitive credentials in environment variables or dedicated secrets management systems like HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager - Implement exponential backoff with jitter for handling API rate limits, starting with a 1-second delay and doubling on each retry up to a maximum of 5 attempts - Use Telegram's setWebhook method with a secret token parameter to validate that incoming requests actually came from Telegram's servers - For OAuth2 integration, implement token refresh logic that proactively refreshes tokens 5 minutes before expiration to prevent mid-operation failures - Create separate bot tokens for development and production environments to isolate issues and prevent accidental notifications to users - Implement proper logging for all external API calls, including request/response metadata, but never log actual credentials or sensitive message content - Use structured logging formats (JSON) to enable easy parsing and alerting based on external service interactions - Configure appropriate timeout values for external API calls (typically 30 seconds) and implement circuit breaker patterns for resilience - When sending messages to Telegram, implement message batching for multiple recipients to respect API rate limits - For Gmail integration, always provide both plain text and HTML versions of emails to ensure compatibility across email clients - Implement health checks that verify external service connectivity and include these in your agent's status reporting - Use webhook endpoints with HTTPS only, as Telegram and Gmail API require secure connections for security compliance

# Example 1: Telegram Bot Setup and Daily Summary Skill
# This example demonstrates complete Telegram integration with OpenClaw

import os
import json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from openclaw import Agent, ToolInterface, skill

class TelegramTool(ToolInterface):
    """OpenClaw Tool Interface for Telegram Bot API integration."""
    
    def __init__(self, bot_token: str, allowed_chat_ids: list = None):
        self.bot_token = bot_token
        self.allowed_chat_ids = allowed_chat_ids or []
        self.base_url = f"https://api.telegram.org/bot{bot_token}"
    
    def configure(self, config: dict):
        """Configure the tool with provided settings."""
        self.webhook_url = config.get("webhook_url")
        self.admin_chat_id = config.get("admin_chat_id")
    
    def initialize(self):
        """Initialize connection and set up webhook."""
        # Set the webhook endpoint for receiving messages
        webhook_endpoint = f"{self.webhook_url}/webhook/telegram"
        response = self._make_request("setWebhook", {
            "url": webhook_endpoint,
            "allowed_updates": ["message", "callback_query"]
        })
        return response.get("ok", False)
    
    def send_message(self, chat_id: int, text: str, parse_mode: str = "Markdown") -> dict:
        """Send a message to a specific Telegram chat."""
        if chat_id not in self.allowed_chat_ids and chat_id != self.admin_chat_id:
            raise PermissionError(f"Chat ID {chat_id} not authorized")
        
        return self._make_request("sendMessage", {
            "chat_id": chat_id,
            "text": text,
            "parse_mode": parse_mode
        })
    
    def _make_request(self, method: str, data: dict) -> dict:
        """Internal method to make API requests to Telegram."""
        import requests
        url = f"{self.base_url}/{method}"
        response = requests.post(url, json=data, timeout=30)
        response.raise_for_status()
        return response.json()

    """Post a daily summary to the configured chat room.
    
    This skill is triggered daily at 9 AM and compiles information
    from various sources to create a comprehensive daily report.

    # Compile summary data from various sources
    summary_data = await gather_daily_metrics(context)
    
    # Format the message with emojis for visual appeal
    summary_text = f"""
📊 *Daily Summary - {datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}*

🔹 *System Status*: {summary_data['status']}
🔹 *Tasks Completed*: {summary_data['tasks_completed']}
🔹 *Errors Encountered*: {summary_data['errors']}
🔹 *Active Users*: {summary_data['active_users']}

📝 *Highlights*:
{chr(10).join(f"  • {item}" for item in summary_data['highlights'])}

⏰ *Next Scheduled Task*: {summary_data['next_task']}
    """
    
    # Send to the configured admin chat
    await telegram_tool.send_message(
        chat_id=telegram_tool.admin_chat_id,
        text=summary_text.strip()
    )

async def gather_daily_metrics(context: dict) -> dict:
    """Gather metrics from various sources for the summary."""
    # Placeholder - in production, this would query actual data sources
    return {
        "status": "🟢 Operational",
        "tasks_completed": 42,
        "errors": 0,
        "active_users": 15,
        "highlights": [
            "Completed automated backup sequence",
            "Processed 1,247 incoming emails",
            "Generated weekly performance report"
        ],
        "next_task": "Weekly cleanup at 18:00"
    }

# Example usage in agent initialization
def setup_telegram_agent():
    """Initialize an agent with Telegram integration."""
    agent = Agent(name="DailyReporter")
    
    # Register the Telegram tool with credentials from environment
    telegram_tool = TelegramTool(
        bot_token=os.environ["TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN"],
        allowed_chat_ids=[int(x) for x in os.environ.get("ALLOWED_CHAT_IDS", "").split(",")]
    )
    
    # Configure the tool
    telegram_tool.configure({
        "webhook_url": os.environ["WEBHOOK_BASE_URL"],
        "admin_chat_id": int(os.environ["ADMIN_CHAT_ID"])
    })
    
    # Initialize and register the tool
    if telegram_tool.initialize():
        agent.register_tool("telegram", telegram_tool)
    
    # Register the skill
    agent.register_skill(post_daily_summary)
    
    return agent
